title: Nonequilibrium Green Functions Simulations for Large Correlated Systems
authors: Erik Schroedter, Michael Bonitz, Jan-Philip Joost
abstract: Correlated real-time dynamics in large, spatially inhomogeneous quantum systems remain difficult to access with nonequilibrium many-body methods. Two-time nonequilibrium Green functions (NEGF) retain dynamical correlations but their computational runtime grows cubically with the number of time steps $N_\mathrm{t}$. This scaling bottleneck could recently be overcome by introducing the G1--G2 scheme that is linear in $N_\mathrm{t}$, but requires propagation of a two-particle correlation function and may suffer from numerical instabilities. This has restricted simulations to small systems with $N_\mathrm{b} \sim 10^2$ basis states. Here we introduce a quantum-fluctuation formulation of nonequilibrium Green functions, denoted $δ$NEGF, that represents dynamical two-particle correlations through fluctuations of field-operator products, $δ\hat G$. This guarantees stable dynamics by preserving the positivity of the reduced density matrices, avoids the explicit storage of the two-particle Green function, and reduces the propagation to a finite ensemble of Hartree-Fock-like trajectories. Combined with a stochastic low-rank decomposition of the correlation functions, the method retains time-linear scaling while extending dynamical $GW$ and particle-particle and particle-hole $T$-matrix simulations to basis sizes of order $N_\mathrm{b}\sim 10^4$. We benchmark $δ$NEGF against exact and HF-GKBA results for lattice systems, finding stable correlated dynamics also at strong coupling. We further demonstrate large-scale simulations of diffusion in two-dimensional Hubbard lattices and ultrafast relaxation in graphene nanoribbon heterostructures with long-range Coulomb interactions. These results establish $δ$NEGF as a scalable route to dynamical self-energy simulations of large, spatially inhomogeneous correlated quantum systems beyond the reach of existing NEGF implementations.
summary: This paper introduces $\delta$NEGF, a novel quantum-fluctuation formulation for Nonequilibrium Green Functions. It solves the critical scaling bottleneck faced by previous methods when simulating correlated systems over long times or large unit cells. This breakthrough enables accurate study of complex dynamics in materials like Hubbard lattices and graphene nanoribbons.
main_problem: The primary challenge is accessing accurate, real-time dynamics for large, spatially inhomogeneous quantum systems where electron correlations are strong.
main_result: The proposed $\delta$NEGF method successfully extends dynamical self-energy simulations to basis sizes of $N_b \sim 10^4$, overcoming the severe scaling bottlenecks of previous NEGF implementations.
method: It introduces a quantum-fluctuation formulation ($\delta$NEGF) that represents two-particle correlations via fluctuations of field operators, avoiding explicit storage of the full two-particle Green function.
model_or_system: The theory applies to general correlated fermionic systems, benchmarked on lattice models like the 2D Hubbard lattice and graphene nanoribbon heterostructures with long-range Coulomb interactions.
key_observables: Dynamical self-energies (GW, T-matrix), charge migration dynamics, and ultrafast relaxation processes.
important_parameters: Basis size ($N_b$) up to $10^4$, time steps ($N_t$), coupling strength (strong coupling regime).
main_assumptions: The method relies on a quantum-fluctuation formulation that guarantees stable dynamics by preserving the positivity of reduced density matrices, and uses stochastic low-rank decomposition for efficiency.
paper_structure: The paper builds upon existing NEGF frameworks (like G1–G2) to address computational scaling limits. It introduces $\delta$NEGF by reformulating correlations through fluctuations ($\delta \hat{G}$), which allows for time-linear scaling and large basis sizes, culminating in benchmarks on physical systems.
why_interesting: It provides a scalable theoretical tool to study non-equilibrium dynamics in strongly correlated materials that were previously computationally inaccessible due to the exponential growth of required correlation functions.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: Figure 1. Levels of self-consistency for the different approaches. Here, “fully self-consistent” refers to the self-consistent solu- tion of the KBEs, for any of the discussed self-energy ap- proximations. Ξ denotes the contribution arising from the collision term inside the integral term of the BSE (18) and is of the form Ξ ∼ R IGwχ. Application of the GKBA is equivalent to neglecting Ξ. Within the δNEGF approach, the R term is neglected what constitutes an intermediate level of self-consistency. Finally, “mean-field propagators” refers to inserting single-particle NEGFs at the mean-field level into the BSE (“one-shot” scheme).

Figure 3. Further analysis of the setup of Fig. 2 for a broad range of coupling strengths, U/J = 0 . . . 20. Asymptotic values of (a) imbalance (97) and (c) correlated double occupation (81), averaged between t1J/ℏ= 20 and t2J/ℏ= 50. (b): first time the imbalance crosses zero.

Figure 2. Benchmarking of δTPP for the melting of an 11-site CDW chain at [(b)–(c)] U/J = 5 and [(d)–(e)] U/J = 15. Comparison of [(b) and (d)] the density imbalance, Eq. (97) and [(c) and (e)] the correlated double occupation, Eq. (81).

Figure 4. Diffusion of a doubly-occupied circular initial distribution containing 74 particles on a 19×19 square lattice for U/J = 3. (a)–(d): Surface plots of the local density distribution at different times for exact simulations which require Ns = 106 345 samples. (e)–(h): Surface plots of the local density distribution at the final time tJ/ℏ= 10, for different numbers of samples Ns. Already for 3200 samples [panel (g)] the results are indistinguishable from the exact result shown in panel (h).

Figure 5. Further analysis of the setup of Fig. 4 for (a) the expansion velocity of the distribution, Eq. (98b), and (b) the correlation energy. Results for TDHF (red) and GKBA+TPP (orange) are added for comparison. Notice that averaged single- particle quantities, such as the expansion velocity, converge much faster than the local density in Fig. 4 and the correlation energy in panel (b). In general, even small sample numbers, cf. curve for Ns = 32, give accurate results.
